title: শিল্পীৰ কোনো ধৰ্ম নাই, কৰ্মহে আচল ধৰ্মঃ পদ্মশ্ৰী নুৰুদ্দিন আহমেদ
description:
ডিজিটেল সংবাদ, নগাওঁঃ নগাঁও জিলা সাহিত্য সভাৰ প্ৰতিষ্ঠাপক সভাপতি, বিশিষ্ট শিক্ষাবিদ তথা অসম লোকসেৱা আয়োগৰ প্ৰাক্তন সদস্য প্ৰয়াত হৰেশ্বৰ গোস্বামীৰ ২৯ সংখ্যক স্মৃতি দিৱস । এই স্মৃতি দিৱস অনুষ্ঠানত  উপস্থিত থাকে বিশিষ্ট চিত্ৰশিল্পী তথা ভাস্কৰ্যশিল্পী নুৰুদ্দিন আহমেদ।
নগাঁৱৰ কলংপাৰ বিদ্যাপীঠ ছিনিয়ৰ চেকেণ্ডেৰী স্কুলৰ উদ্যোগত আয়োজিত এক গাম্ভীৰ্যপূৰ্ণ স্মাৰক বক্তৃতা অনুষ্ঠানত মুখ্য অতিথি হিচাপে উপস্থিত থাকি শিল্পীগৰাকীয়ে  মন্তব্য প্ৰদান কৰে। তেওঁ কয় যে, "মুছলমানসকলে ছবি-মূৰ্তি আঁকিব নাপায় বুলি সৰুতে আমাক শিকোৱা হৈছিল। কিন্তু ডাঙৰ হৈ বুজিছিলোঁ যে শিল্পীৰ কোনো ধৰ্ম নাই। শিল্পকৰ্মৰ লগতো ধৰ্মৰ কোনো সম্পৰ্ক নাই; কৰ্মইহে আচল ধৰ্ম।"
উল্লেখ্য যে, নিজকে এটা অতি দুখীয়া পৰিয়ালৰ সন্তান বুলি উল্লেখ কৰি পদ্মশ্ৰী নুৰুদ্দিন আহমেদে কয় যে- তেওঁ কেতিয়াও কোনো সন্মান লাভৰ আশাত কাম কৰা নাই, কেৱল কাম কৰাৰ আনন্দৰ বাবেই সৃষ্টিশীল সাধনাত মগ্ন হৈছে। তেওঁ আৱেগিকভাৱে প্ৰকাশ কৰে যে, মহাপুৰুষ শ্ৰীমন্ত শংকৰদেৱৰ পবিত্ৰ জন্মস্থান বটদ্ৰৱা থানৰ আৱিৰ্ভাৱ ক্ষেত্ৰত গুৰু-আসন, কৃষ্ণলীলা আৰু বিষ্ণুৰ দশাৱতাৰৰ ভাস্কৰ্য নিৰ্মাণ কৰিবলৈ পোৱাতো তেওঁৰ জীৱনলৈ ভগৱানৰ শ্ৰেষ্ঠতম উপহাৰ।

এই কাৰ্যসূচীত তেওঁৰ সহযোগী ভাস্কৰ তথা দুই পুত্ৰ ৰাজ আহমেদ আৰু দীপ আহমেদকো সম্বৰ্ধনা জনোৱা হয়। পিতৃৰ পদাংক অনুসৰণ কৰি দুই পুত্ৰয়ো কয় যে-জন্মভূমি অসমৰ বুকুত শংকৰদেৱ গুৰুজনক তেওঁলোকেও গভীৰ শ্ৰদ্ধা কৰে আৰু বটদ্ৰৱা প্ৰকল্পত কাম কৰিবলৈ পোৱাটো তেওঁলোকৰ পৰম সৌভাগ্য।
আনফালে, অসম সাহিত্য সভাৰ উপ-সভাপতি পদুম ৰাজখোৱাই আঁত ধৰা এই অনুষ্ঠানত "ছাত্ৰ-ছাত্ৰীসকলৰ মাজত শংকৰদেৱ অধ্যয়ন আৰু ইয়াৰ প্ৰয়োজনীয়তা" শীৰ্ষক স্মাৰক বক্তৃতা প্ৰদান কৰে নগাঁও বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ৰ শিক্ষয়িত্ৰী ড° কৰবী দেৱীয়ে। তেওঁ কয় যে- "আজি বিশ্বই শ্বেইকছপীয়েৰ বা শ্বেলীৰ গুণানুকীৰ্তন কৰে, কিন্তু আমাৰ শ্ৰীমন্ত শংকৰদেৱ তেওঁলোকতকৈ কোনোগুণে কম নাছিল। তেওঁ কেৱল ধৰ্ম প্ৰচাৰক বা নাট্যকাৰেই নাছিল, বৰঞ্চ এক অদ্বিতীয় প্ৰতিভা আছিল। অথচ আমাৰ মাজত জাতীয় প্ৰেমৰ অভাৱ থকাৰ বাবেই আমি গুৰুজনক বিশ্বদৰবাৰত প্ৰতিষ্ঠা কৰিবলৈ অসমৰ্থ হৈছোঁ।"
ভাষণৰ মাজতে বৰগীত পৰিৱেশন কৰি তেওঁ অসমীয়া যুৱপ্ৰজন্মক কৰ্মসংস্কৃতি গঢ়াৰ আন্দোলন কৰিবলৈ আৰু শিশুসকলক শংকৰদেৱ অধ্যয়নৰ বাবে উৎসাহিত কৰিবলৈ আহ্বান জনায়।

এিখিনিতে উল্লেখ কৰিব লাগিব যে, প্ৰাক্তন বিধায়ক গিৰীন্দ্ৰকুমাৰ বৰুৱাৰ পৌৰোহিত্যত অনুষ্ঠিত এই অনুষ্ঠানৰ আৰম্ভণিতে শিক্ষাবিদ হৰেশ্বৰ গোস্বামীৰ প্ৰতিচ্ছবিত শ্ৰদ্ধাঞ্জলি অৰ্পণ কৰা হয়। বন্তি প্ৰজ্বলন কৰে শিক্ষাবিদগৰাকীৰ সন্তান ক্ৰমে সপোন গোস্বামী, সঞ্জীৱ গোস্বামী আৰু স্নিগ্ধা গোস্বামীয়ে। পুষ্পাঞ্জলি তৰ্পণ কৰে গোৱিন্দ কলিতা, সুশীল ডেকা, শ্যামন্ত দাসে। এই অনুষ্ঠানতে নগাঁৱৰ সমাজ জীৱনৰ প্ৰায় ৩০ গৰাকী বিশিষ্ট ব্যক্তিক বিশেষভাৱে সন্মান জনোৱা হয়।
ইপিনে, সভাপতিৰ ভাষণত প্ৰাক্তন অধ্যাপক গিৰীন্দ্ৰকুমাৰ বৰুৱাই প্ৰয়াত হৰেশ্বৰ গোস্বামীক ‘সমাজৰ বিবেক’ বুলি আখ্যা দিয়ে। ইয়াৰ লগতে তেওঁ সমগ্ৰ অসমবাসীক এক গুৰুত্বপূৰ্ণ আহ্বান জনাই কয়-"জাতি-ধৰ্ম-বৰ্ণ নিৰ্বিশেষে অসমক ভালপোৱা প্ৰতিগৰাকী লোকে নিজক অসমীয়া বুলি পৰিচয় দি গৌৰৱ কৰক আৰু আগন্তুক লোকপিয়লত অসমীয়াক নিজৰ প্ৰথম ভাষা হিচাপে স্বীকৃতি দিয়ক।"
পৰিয়ালৰ হৈ জ্যেষ্ঠ পুত্ৰ সপোন গোস্বামীয়ে পিতৃৰ স্মৃতি ৰক্ষাৰ বাবে কলংপাৰ বিদ্যাপীঠে কৰি অহা ২৯ বছৰীয়া প্ৰয়াসৰ শলাগ লয়। সামৰণিত বিদ্যালয়ৰ শিক্ষক দিগন্ত বৰদলৈ, জ্যোতিমণি ডেকা, বিজয় বসুমতাৰী, আব্দুল ৱাহিদ আদিয়ে পৰিৱেশন কৰা জাতীয় সংগীতেৰে এই ত্ৰিশতাধিক লোক উপস্থিত থকা সভাৰ সামৰণি পৰে।

title: NextSTEP-3 A: Lunar Enabling Technology
description: Solicitation Number: 80GRC026R0008 May 19, 2026 – Synopsis issued June 29, 2026 – Draft BAA and Appendix A Issued | News Release   NASA issued a draft Broad Agency Announcement under NextSTEP‑3, Appendix A, on June 29, 2026, to advance concepts that accelerate the technological readiness of critical systems for lunar surface and cislunar architecture.  This solicitation seeks to close key technology gaps and […]
content:
1 min readPreparations for Next Moonwalk Simulations Underway (and Underwater)


Solicitation Number: 80GRC026R0008 May 19, 2026 – Synopsis issued June 29, 2026 – Draft BAA and Appendix A Issued | News Release  


Artistic concept of lunar surface technologies and infrastructure capabilities, including in-situ resource utilization oxygen production systems, surface power systems, in‑space manufacturing tools, and advanced nanomaterials production.NASA


NASA issued a draft Broad Agency Announcement under NextSTEP‑3, Appendix A, on June 29, 2026, to advance concepts that accelerate the technological readiness of critical systems for lunar surface and cislunar architecture. 



This solicitation seeks to close key technology gaps and mature capabilities in vertical solar arrays, ISRU oxygen production systems, Stirling radioisotope generators, in‑space manufacturing, and advanced nanomaterials production. 



It focuses on identifying technology areas that require further risk reduction and ground‑based testing to mature competing solutions to Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 5–6. Funded efforts will advance the technology objectives of NASA’s Moon Base by demonstrating critical systems and accelerating the development of transformative capabilities needed for near‑term mission success. 



For more information, read the Lunar Enabling Infrastructure Accelerator (LEIA) Broad Agency Announcement (BAA) NextSTEP-3 Appendix A – Draft Solicitation on SAM.gov.

title: NASA Seeks Industry Input to Accelerate Lunar Surface Technologies
description: Long-term lunar exploration requires technology, infrastructure, and operations that function together cohesively on the surface of the Moon. To accelerate the development of key lunar surface systems and reduce risk, NASA and industry must work together in the design, development, testing, and evaluation of innovative solutions that support U.S. space priorities.  NASA is seeking feedback on a draft solicitation for the Lunar […]
content:
3 min readPreparations for Next Moonwalk Simulations Underway (and Underwater)

Artistic concept of lunar surface technologies and infrastructure capabilities, including in-situ resource utilization oxygen production systems, surface power systems, in‑space manufacturing tools, and advanced nanomaterials production.NASA


Long-term lunar exploration requires technology, infrastructure, and operations that function together cohesively on the surface of the Moon. To accelerate the development of key lunar surface systems and reduce risk, NASA and industry must work together in the design, development, testing, and evaluation of innovative solutions that support U.S. space priorities. 



NASA is seeking feedback on a draft solicitation for the Lunar Enabling Infrastructure Accelerator, an effort to help develop emerging capabilities in surface power, in-situ resource utilization, advanced manufacturing, and innovative nanomaterials. The draft is available for review by U.S. organizations, including industry, educational institutions, and non-profits.



		
			
				  
			
			
				
					Investments in space technology development unlock the near-impossible for NASA and the nation. A sustained human presence at the Moon requires breakthrough ideas from a competitive U.S industrial base, and we are proud to work toward that vision with our commercial partners.
				
				
					
					
						Greg Stover
						Director of the Advanced Research and Technology Division, Research and Technology Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington
					
				
			
		
	


This review period allows NASA an opportunity to gather feedback on the draft solicitation, including the requirements, schedules, proposal instructions, and evaluation approaches. NASA strongly encourages industry to carefully review the draft and identify any areas of ambiguity, or concerns. Industry input will help inform the solicitation’s final requirements, acquisition planning, and solicitation parameters.



The Lunar Enabling Infrastructure Accelerator includes five topics that address gaps in technology needed for exploring the Moon and the cislunar region between Earth and the Moon as identified in NASA’s Civil Space Shortfalls. The topics focus on near-term mission priorities:



Surface power: Access to continuous, localized, and scalable power generation throughout the lunar day and night is essential for initial phases of the Moon Base plan. NASA’s needs include power generation, power management and distribution, and energy storage.



Radioisotope power: A type of nuclear energy technology that uses heat to produce electric power for operating spacecraft systems in the darkest, dustiest, and most remote places in our solar system.  



In-situ resource utilization: As a sustained presence grows at the Moon, opportunities to harvest lunar resources could lead to safer, more efficient operations with less dependence on Earth. Advancing in-situ resource utilization technologies could support production of fuel, water, and oxygen from local materials, expanding exploration capabilities. 



In-space advanced manufacturing: Long-term human presence beyond Earth orbit requires autonomous in-space production of essential tools and materials. Advancing in-space manufacturing will be critical to reducing reliance on Earth resupply, as well as optimizing mission flexibility and resilience at the Moon, Mars, and elsewhere in deep space. 



Innovative nanomaterials: U.S. objectives related to the commercialization of low Earth orbit, building a sustained presence on the lunar surface, and pursuing deeper space exploration will involve work in demanding operational environments and under stringent mission constraints. To meet the agency’s most ambitious space exploration goals, this topic seeks to advance the commercial availability, performance, quality, and uniformity of nanomaterials to address environmental, mass, and performance challenges. 



Lunar Enabling Infrastructure Accelerator awardees will be expected to design, develop, and demonstrate prototype systems and generate validated performance data, analytical models, and operational insights through testing and demonstration activities to mature technology and manufacturing applications.  



The solicitation, Next Space Technologies for Exploration Partnerships-3 (NextSTEP-3) Appendix A Lunar Enabling Infrastructure Accelerator (Solicitation No: 80GRC026R0008), is available on SAM.gov and is open for comment through July 17, 2026.



For more information about NASA’s space technology website as a reference for current technology strategy and priorities, visit:



https://www.nasa.gov/resources/

title: आपूर्ति श्रृंखला पर विदेशी व्यापारियों का विश्वास मजबूत
description:
चौथा चीन अंतर्राष्ट्रीय आपूर्ति श्रृंखला संवर्धन एक्सपो (CISCE) 22 से 26 जून तक पेइचिंग में आयोजित हुआ। इसमें 600 से अधिक उद्यमों और विदेशी सरकारी प्रतिनिधिमंडलों ने भाग लिया। उनकी नजर में चीन की आपूर्ति श्रृंखला न सिर्फ विभिन्न देशों के बाजारों को जोड़ती है, बल्कि द्विपक्षीय व बहुपक्षीय आर्थिक व व्यापारिक सहयोग के लिए.
The post आपूर्ति श्रृंखला पर विदेशी व्यापारियों का विश्वास मजबूत appeared first on Dainik Savera Times.
content:
चौथा चीन अंतर्राष्ट्रीय आपूर्ति श्रृंखला संवर्धन एक्सपो (CISCE) 22 से 26 जून तक पेइचिंग में आयोजित हुआ। इसमें 600 से अधिक उद्यमों और विदेशी सरकारी प्रतिनिधिमंडलों ने भाग लिया। उनकी नजर में चीन की आपूर्ति श्रृंखला न सिर्फ विभिन्न देशों के बाजारों को जोड़ती है, बल्कि द्विपक्षीय व बहुपक्षीय आर्थिक व व्यापारिक सहयोग के लिए विशाल संभावना देती है।
निकारागुआ के राष्ट्रपति के सलाहकार लॉरेनो ओर्टेगा मुरिलो ने कहा कि हमने पहली बार CISCE में भाग लिया और यह आखिरी बार नहीं होगा। आशा है कि हर साल हम CISCE में भाग ले सकते हैं। चीन और निकारागुआ के बीच कई सहयोग परियोजनाएं लागू हो रही हैं। बहुत से चीनी उद्यम निकारागुआ में व्यापार कर रहे हैं। हमें निकारागुआ में निर्मित हो रही परियोजनाओं की आपूर्ति श्रृंखला की स्थिरता सुनिश्चित करनी होगी। यह CISCE में हमारी भागीदारी का मुख्य कारण है।
सर्बिया की व्यापार मंत्री जगोडा लाज़ारेविक ने कहा कि बुद्धिमान आपूर्ति श्रृंखला जैसा कि आधुनिक विनिर्माण, औद्योगिक रोबोट और कई अत्याधुनिक नई प्रौद्योगिकी में चीन दुनिया में आगे है। सर्बिया समेत अन्य देशों को न सिर्फ पीछा करना होगा, बल्कि सीखना भी होगा।
चीन स्थित ऑस्ट्रेलिया के वाणिज्यिक दूत डोमिनिक ट्रिनिटी ने कहा कि ऑस्ट्रेलिया ने मुख्य अतिथि देश के रूप में चौथे CISCE में भाग लिया। हम यह सूचना देना चाहता है कि ऑस्ट्रेलिया न केवल उच्च गुणवत्ता वाले स्वच्छ और हरित उत्पाद दे सकता है, बल्कि आपूर्ति श्रृंखला में चीन का विश्वसनीय साझेदार भी है।
चीन में जर्मन चैंबर ऑफ कॉमर्स के बोर्ड के सदस्य ओलिवर ओहम्स ने कहा कि चीन में जर्मन व्यापारियों का विश्वास पिछले साल से भी ऊंचा है। चौथे CISCE ने संकेत दिया है कि विभिन्न जगतों को वैश्विक आपूर्ति श्रृंखला पर विश्वास होना चाहिए।
(साभार- चाइना मीडिया ग्रुप, पेइचिंग)
The post आपूर्ति श्रृंखला पर विदेशी व्यापारियों का विश्वास मजबूत appeared first on Dainik Savera Times.

title: Bribery News : घरकुलाच्या दुसर्‍या हप्त्यासाठी हजाराची लाच
description: वैजापूर |प्रतिनिधी| Vaijapur पंचायत समितीमध्ये (Vaijapur Panchayat Samiti) कार्यरत ग्रामीण गृहनिर्माण अभियंत्याला (Rural Housing Engineer) पंतप्रधान घरकुल आवास योजनेअंतर्गत मंजूर घरकुलाचा दुसरा हप्ता (Gharkul Installment) मंजूर करून देण्यासाठी एक हजार रुपयांची लाच (Bribe) स्वीकारताना लाचलुचपत प्रतिबंधक (एसीबी) विभागाने सोमवारी (29 जून) रंगेहाथ अटक केली. प्रवीण गायकवाड (वय 35, रा. पानवी, ता. वैजापूर) असे अटक करण्यात […]
content:
वैजापूर |प्रतिनिधी| Vaijapur 



पंचायत समितीमध्ये (Vaijapur Panchayat Samiti) कार्यरत ग्रामीण गृहनिर्माण अभियंत्याला (Rural Housing Engineer) पंतप्रधान घरकुल आवास योजनेअंतर्गत मंजूर घरकुलाचा दुसरा हप्ता (Gharkul Installment) मंजूर करून देण्यासाठी एक हजार रुपयांची लाच (Bribe) स्वीकारताना लाचलुचपत प्रतिबंधक (एसीबी) विभागाने सोमवारी (29 जून) रंगेहाथ अटक केली. 



प्रवीण गायकवाड (वय 35, रा. पानवी, ता. वैजापूर) असे अटक करण्यात आलेल्या अभियंत्याचे नाव आहे. 31 वर्षीय तक्रारदार यांना पंतप्रधान घरकुल आवास योजनेअंतर्गत (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana) घरकुल मंजूर झाले असून पहिला हप्ता प्राप्त झाला होता. दुसरा हप्ता मिळण्यासाठी आवश्यक असलेली स्थळ पाहणी, जिओ-टॅगिंग व छायाचित्रे अपलोड करण्यासाठी त्यांनी संबंधित अभियंत्याकडे वारंवार पाठपुरावा केला. मात्र, दुसरा हप्ता मंजूर करून देण्यासाठी आरोपी अभियंत्याने एक हजार रुपयांच्या लाचेची (Bribe) मागणी केली.



तक्रारदार यांची लाच देण्याची तयारी नसल्याने त्यांनी छत्रपती संभाजीनगर येथील लाचलुचपत प्रतिबंधक विभागाकडे (Anti-Corruption Department) तक्रार दाखल केली. तक्रारीची पडताळणी केली असता, घरकुलाच्या दुसर्‍या हप्त्याचे 75 हजार रुपयांचे बिल काढून देण्यासाठी आरोपीने एक हजार रुपयांची मागणी केल्याचे निष्पन्न झाले. यानंतर एसीबीने सोमवारी पंचायत समिती कार्यालयात सापळा रचला. तक्रारदारांकडून प्रवीण गायकवाड याने एक हजार रुपयांची लाच (Bribe) स्वीकारताच पथकाने त्याला पंचांसमक्ष ताब्यात घेतले. कारवाईदरम्यान त्याच्याकडून लाचेची एक हजार रुपयांची रक्कम, अन्य 450 रुपये रोख तसेच दोन मोबाईल जप्त (Seized) करण्यात आले.



ही कारवाई पोलिस अधीक्षक माधुरी कांगणे, अप्पर पोलिस अधीक्षक शशिकांत सिंगारे आणि पोलिस उपअधीक्षक प्रशांत पवार यांच्या मार्गदर्शनाखाली पोलिस निरीक्षक शांतीलाल चव्हाण, पोलिस हवालदार रवींद्र काळे, युवराज हिवाळे, अशोक नागरगोजे, चांगदेव बागुल आणि रामेश्वर ताठे यांच्या पथकाने केली. प्रकरणाचा पुढील तपास पोलिस निरीक्षक प्रतिभा अबूज करीत आहेत.

title: Comcast is splitting its media and broadband properties
description: NBCUniversal and Sky will be spun off into separate companies.
content:
Comcast said it plans to separate its media businesses from its mobile and broadband networks in the latest reshaping of the US industry, sending shares in the group up more than 20 percent on Monday.
The US media group said it expected to complete the break-up within a year through a tax-free spin-off of NBCUniversal and Sky —handing existing shareholders stock in both Comcast and the new standalone media company.
The move comes as the traditional American media industry races to keep pace as audiences shift their attention to social media and streaming platforms.Read full article

title: A worthy P1 successor? 1258bhp, RWD McLaren W1 driven on road and track
description:
McLaren introduces its third '1' car in its history, with almost 1300bhp and a £2 million price tag

There is a recent trend for hypercars to have four-wheel drive, with engines typically in their middle and supplemented by electric motors at the front.And with good reason, because more than 1000bhp is an awful lot for two wheels to deal with by themselves. Some engineers say that to get the best out of so much power, it must be distributed through four tyres.McLaren engineers don’t feel the same. This is the W1, McLaren’s latest Ultimate Series hypercar and the third in the lineage of ‘1’ cars, after the F1 and P1 (and only the second ‘1’ during the new iteration of McLaren’s road car division). Like those, it is carbonfibre-tubbed and mid-engined – and exclusively rear-wheel drive.As with the P1 it’s a hybrid, but this time it can’t be plugged in, because that would make it heavier. McLaren has taken a different path from some of its competitors by attempting to rule out anything that adds unnecessary weight.So, as with equipping it with rear-wheel drive only, it has opted for a mechanical layout that makes the car as light as is realistically possible. McLaren claims a lightest dry weight of 1399kg (I’d prefer a wet kerb weight, but rivals use this number too). Anyway, you can imagine how hard they will have worked for the last kilo.Here’s how it pans out.

title: Xiaomi, Apple, Google Reportedly Join Hands to Advance Qi 50W Wireless Charging Standard
description: Xiaomi reportedly hosts key meeting on the Qi 50W charging standard
content: Xiaomi has hosted a Wireless Power Consortium meeting in Beijing to advance the upcoming Qi 50W wireless charging standard, according to a recent report. Apple, Google, Huawei, Oppo, Vivo, and several other companies participated in discussions, prototype testing, and interoperability verification. The report says the standard is expected to launch in 2028 after further development. Xiaomi is also promoting a small inductance, low voltage, and high power charging architecture that has already entered the Qi 50W standard drafting process.

title: How Abdulaziz Osman is building the infrastructure layer behind the digital economy
description: The next decade will not be defined solely by software, artificial intelligence, or digital assets.It will be defined by the infrastructure powering them.Behind every artificial intelligence model, every cloud application, every digital payment, and every blockchain transaction lies a vast ecosystem of energy systems, data centers, computing hardware, networking infrastructure, and operational expertise.As the global economy becomes increasingly digital, the organizations building this infrastructure are becoming some of the most strategically important businesses in the world.Among the companies operating at the intersection of this transformation is BitHash, a UAE-based digital infrastructure company founded by entrepreneur Abdulaziz Osman.Over the past several years, BitHash has evolved from a digital asset infrastructure operator into a broader platform focused on computing infrastructure, renewable energy integration, hosting solutions, and next-generation digital infrastructure ecosystems.Today, the company reports:50 MW deployed or under developmentMore than 10,000 ASIC miners deployedOperations across five global locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW major infrastructure milestones achievedWhile these numbers reflect significant growth, Abdulaziz Osman believes they represent only the beginning of a much larger opportunity."We are entering a new infrastructure cycle," he says."Artificial intelligence, data centers, digital assets, and energy are converging into a single ecosystem. The organizations that build and operate this infrastructure will help define the future global economy."Chapter One: The Entrepreneurial JourneyEvery entrepreneur's story begins long before their company is founded.For Abdulaziz Osman, the journey began with a simple observation:The world's greatest opportunities are often hidden beneath the surface.Like many ambitious professionals, Osman began his career in the corporate world.The environment offered stability, structure, and predictability.Yet as global technological transformation accelerated, he found himself increasingly drawn toward entrepreneurship.What fascinated him was not simply technology itself.It was the infrastructure behind it.Throughout history, every major economic transformation has depended upon infrastructure.Railroads powered industrialization.Electricity transformed manufacturing.Telecommunications connected nations.The internet reshaped commerce.Cloud computing revolutionized enterprise technology.Infrastructure always came first.The products and services that captured public attention were built upon foundations created years earlier.This realization would ultimately become the foundation upon which BitHash was built."The biggest opportunities often exist where few people are looking," Osman explains."Everyone sees the finished product. Few people focus on the infrastructure making it possible."Leaving corporate life was not easy.Entrepreneurship required risk.It required uncertainty.It required resilience.Like most founders, Osman encountered setbacks, unexpected challenges, and moments when progress seemed slow.Yet those experiences reinforced a philosophy that continues guiding him today:Consistency beats intensity.Discipline beats motivation.Execution beats theory."Success rarely comes from one breakthrough moment," he says."It comes from showing up every day, solving problems, and continuing forward when outcomes remain uncertain."Chapter Two: Seeing Beyond BitcoinWhen many people hear the phrase Bitcoin mining, they immediately think of cryptocurrency.They think about price charts.Market cycles.Volatility.Speculation.Abdulaziz Osman sees something entirely different.He sees infrastructure.Behind every Bitcoin transaction exists a sophisticated ecosystem of energy systems, networking architecture, cooling technologies, data centers, logistics, and operational management.The deeper Osman studied the industry, the more convinced he became that the true long-term opportunity was not speculation.It was infrastructure.This distinction fundamentally shaped BitHash's strategy.While others focused on market cycles, BitHash focused on building systems.While others followed short-term trends, BitHash concentrated on long-term infrastructure development."Bitcoin mining is no longer simply a cryptocurrency business," Osman says."It is an infrastructure business."This philosophy enabled BitHash to position itself for opportunities extending far beyond digital assets.Infrastructure remains valuable regardless of market cycles.Computing power remains necessary.Energy remains necessary.Data centers remain necessary.The future belongs to those who build and manage these systems efficiently.Chapter Three: The Rise of the Infrastructure EconomyThroughout history, infrastructure has driven economic growth.Today, another infrastructure revolution is underway.Artificial intelligence.Data centers.Cloud computing.Digital assets.Renewable energy.High-performance computing.These sectors increasingly depend upon one another.Together they form what Osman describes as the Infrastructure Economy.Artificial intelligence requires data centers.Data centers require energy.Energy requires infrastructure.Digital assets require computing power.Computing power requires cooling systems and networking architecture.The industries are no longer operating independently.They are converging.Organizations capable of managing these interconnected systems may become some of the most influential companies of the coming decade.This convergence forms the foundation of BitHash's long-term vision.The company does not see itself solely as a mining operator.It sees itself as a digital infrastructure company.One capable of supporting multiple computing ecosystems as demand continues expanding globally.Chapter Four: Artificial Intelligence and the Computing RevolutionFew technologies have generated as much excitement as artificial intelligence.From large language models and autonomous systems to enterprise automation and predictive analytics, AI is transforming nearly every industry.Yet AI is fundamentally an infrastructure story.Training advanced AI models requires enormous computing resources.Deploying those models requires:Data centersHigh-performance computing hardwareNetworking infrastructureCooling technologiesReliable powerEvery advancement in AI increases demand for infrastructure.Industry forecasts suggest AI-related infrastructure investment could exceed hundreds of billions of dollars globally over the coming decade.This transformation presents significant opportunities for infrastructure operators.The expertise required to manage large-scale computing environments increasingly applies across multiple sectors.Whether supporting AI, blockchain, cloud computing, or enterprise applications, the underlying requirements remain remarkably similar.Energy.Computing power.Cooling.Connectivity.Operational excellence.These capabilities form the foundation of BitHash's long-term strategy."We believe AI will become one of the largest infrastructure opportunities of our generation," Osman says."The demand for computing power is only beginning."Chapter Five: Data Centers Become Strategic AssetsIn many ways, data centers have become the factories of the digital age.Every online transaction.Every cloud application.Every AI query.Every enterprise platform.Every digital asset transaction.Depends upon data center infrastructure.Without data centers, the modern digital economy simply cannot function.As digital transformation accelerates globally, institutional investors increasingly view data centers as strategic assets.The sector continues attracting significant investment from:Private equity firmsInfrastructure fundsTechnology companiesSovereign wealth fundsFamily officesThe rationale is straightforward.Computing demand continues growing.Computing demand requires infrastructure.BitHash believes this trend represents one of the defining investment themes of the next decade.The company continues evaluating opportunities within digital infrastructure ecosystems capable of supporting future demand across multiple industries.Chapter Six: Renewable Energy StrategyEnergy is becoming one of the most important competitive advantages in the digital economy.Without energy, computing cannot operate.Without affordable energy, computing cannot scale.Without sustainable energy, long-term growth becomes increasingly difficult.Recognizing this reality, BitHash has incorporated renewable energy into its infrastructure roadmap.The company currently reports 20 MW of renewable energy capacity.For Osman, renewable energy is not simply an environmental initiative.It is an infrastructure strategy.Organizations capable of integrating energy production with computing infrastructure may enjoy significant long-term advantages."Energy will be one of the defining strategic assets of the next decade," Osman says."The companies that successfully integrate infrastructure and energy strategy will be positioned to grow."Chapter Seven: Building at ScaleInfrastructure development requires execution.Vision alone is not enough.Success requires planning.Capital allocation.Operational excellence.Risk management.Over the years, BitHash has focused on building infrastructure capable of supporting long-term growth.Today, the company reports:Key Milestones50 MW deployed or under developmentMore than 10,000 ASIC miners deployedOperations spanning five global locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW major infrastructure milestones achievedThese achievements represent years of deployment, operational management, and infrastructure development.For Osman, however, they are milestones rather than destinations."We are still early in our journey," he says."The infrastructure opportunity ahead is significantly larger than what has already been achieved."Chapter Eight: Global ExpansionThe future of infrastructure is global.Technology moves globally.Capital moves globally.Energy markets operate globally.Computing demand is global.As a result, BitHash has pursued an international growth strategy.Today, the company operates across five global locations.The UAE remains central to this strategy.Its strategic location, business-friendly environment, and commitment to innovation make it an ideal foundation for international expansion.From this base, BitHash continues evaluating opportunities across emerging and established markets.The objective is not simply geographic expansion.It is strategic positioning.Building infrastructure where long-term demand is expected to grow.Chapter Nine: Leadership and CultureEvery organization reflects its leadership.At BitHash, culture is shaped by several core principles:Resilience.Accountability.Execution.Long-term thinking.Entrepreneurship requires navigating uncertainty.Markets evolve.Technology changes.Challenges emerge.Organizations capable of adapting while maintaining focus often achieve sustainable success.According to Osman, culture ultimately determines execution.And execution determines outcomes."Great businesses are not built overnight," he says."They are built through thousands of small decisions made consistently over time."Chapter Ten: Looking Toward the FutureThe world is entering a new infrastructure era.Artificial intelligence continues expanding.Data center demand continues growing.Renewable energy adoption continues accelerating.Digital assets continue maturing.Each trend reinforces the importance of infrastructure.For BitHash, the future lies at the intersection of these industries.The company continues investing in infrastructure development, energy integration, computing ecosystems, and international expansion.Its objective is not merely to participate in technological transformation.Its objective is to help build the systems enabling it."The next generation of digital infrastructure will be built where energy, computing power, and capital intersect," Osman says."The organizations that understand this convergence today will help define the global economy tomorrow."About Abdulaziz OsmanAbdulaziz Osman is the Founder and Chief Executive Officer of BitHash. He is recognized for his work in digital infrastructure, Bitcoin mining, renewable energy integration, data center development, and next-generation computing ecosystems.About BitHashBitHash is a UAE-based digital infrastructure company focused on Bitcoin mining, hosting solutions, renewable energy integration, data center development, and digital infrastructure services.Company Milestones50 MW deployed or under development10,000+ ASIC miners deployed5 global operating locations20 MW renewable energy capacity30 MW infrastructure milestones achievedLinkedin :-   www.linkedin.com/in/abdulaziz-osman-crypto-mining-expertWebsite :-  www.bithash.aeEmail:-  Info@bithash.aeThis is sponsored content and has not been written by Forbes India journalists. Forbes India has not independently verified the information contained herein and makes no representation regarding its accuracy, completeness, or reliability. Readers should make their independent inquiries and take informed decisions.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Industry experts chart the future of India’s bond and fixed income markets
description: India’s fixed income market enters a new era of growth as investors increasingly look for balanced and resilient investment strategies. The market is witnessing increased retail participation, regulatory reforms, and growing relevance in portfolio allocation.As fixed income continues to gain momentum in India’s financial ecosystem, IndiaBonds presents CNBC-TV18 India Fixed Income Summit powered by Mirae Asset Mutual Fund, a platform uniting key voices shaping the future of India’s fixed income markets.The grand event brought together regulators, exchanges, market intermediaries, policymakers, institutional investors and industry leaders to explore emerging trends, investor behaviour and opportunities driving India’s bond markets, while offering expert insights on building a stronger, transparent, inclusive and investor-friendly fixed income ecosystem.From regulatory reforms, technology-led transformation and market accessibility to evolving investment strategies, infrastructure financing and wealth diversification, this summit dives deep into the trends defining the next phase of growth in India’s fixed income ecosystem.Among the event sponsors were IndiaBonds, Mirae Asset Mutual Fund, AK Capital Services, Nippon India Mutual Fund, NaBFID, and NSE, highlighting strong industry support for discussions around the present landscape and future direction of India’s fixed income market.The event commenced with a welcome address by Latha Venkatesh (Consulting Editor, CNBC-TV18), who welcomed the attendees and set the stage for meaningful conversations on India’s fixed income market, bond market development and the future of debt capital markets.The Size and Scale of India’s Debt MarketThe Indian debt market is a critical component of the nation’s financial ecosystem. Its considerable market size not only highlights the maturity and depth of India’s debt instruments but also emphasises the potential for future growth.As of March 31st, 2026, the total size of the Indian debt market stands at approximately US$2.76 trillion, while the world’s bond market stands at a staggering US$145 trillion. It is noteworthy that although India’s bond market is large, there is an immense opportunity for further expansion.According to Aditi Mittal (Co-Founder, IndiaBonds), “India's bond market is undergoing rapid democratisation, driven by growing retail participation and greater accessibility. While significant progress has been made, the real opportunity lies ahead as corporate bonds become accessible to every investor and every demat account.”Bond Markets Powering India’s Growth StoryFor decades, bonds and fixed income largely remained in background conversations. But now, they are emerging as a key focus area in India’s financial ecosystem, driven by technology and infrastructure development.Vishal Goenka (Co-Founder, IndiaBonds) emphasised the need to deepen India’s fixed income ecosystem, expand bond market participation and bring key industry conversations to one stage. In his opening address, he mentions, “a strong and trusted bond market is essential for India's next phase of growth, providing long-term capital for infrastructure, diversified funding for businesses, and broader investment opportunities for households.”Maninder Cheema (Executive Director, SEBI) also shared her perspective on the evolution of India’s bond market. According to her, “The road to building a deeper, more liquid, transparent and globally credible bond market in India begins with establishing trust, Request for Quote (RFQ) platforms, market-making frameworks, online bond platforms, retail participation, and credit derivatives.”Technology Creating Access and TransparencyTechnology has significantly improved retail access to the bond market. It enables real-time portfolio tracking, seamless transactions, and regular price discovery, bringing the bond investing experience closer to that of equities. As a result, bond investing is becoming more transparent, convenient, and accessible to a wider base of retail investors.Besides, the integration of retail online bond platforms is opening up new opportunities for retail investors to access corporate bonds and debt investments. These platforms allow retail investors to seamlessly explore, invest, and trade bonds.Role of Credit Cycle in Shaping Fixed Income StrategiesThe panel explored the current stage of the credit cycle and its implications for India's fixed income markets. The discussion highlighted how evolving funding conditions, sector-specific risks, and changing capital costs are influencing credit spreads, bond pricing, and investment opportunities across the fixed income landscape.Suyash Choudhary (CIO - Fixed Income, Bandhan Mutual Fund) says, “The current credit cycle is becoming increasingly sector-specific, with funding pressures and external shocks affecting industries differently. In such an environment, credit selection and risk assessment become critical for fixed income investors.”Amit Tripathi (CIO - Fixed Income, Nippon India Mutual Fund) adds, “Liquidity in the secondary market for corporate bonds has been a long-standing challenge, but with a well-functioning corporate bond repo ecosystem, there is great potential to improve this. It could make it easier for everyone to hold, finance, buy, and sell these bonds smoothly.”Why Fixed Income Matters Fixed income instruments are gradually evolving beyond traditional FDs, with a wider range of debt mutual fund categories now offering different duration, liquidity and yield options for investors. Despite attractive yields and better liquidity compared to traditional FDs, fixed income allocation remains low among retail investors.According to Basant Bafna (Head of Fixed Income, Mirae Asset Mutual Fund), “Investors can align fixed income investments with financial goals for long-term financial planning. But one of the biggest gaps in the Indian investment landscape is awareness around fixed income products and how they can fit into long-term financial planning.”Indian Bond Market and Way ForwardA K Mittal (MD & CEO, AK Capital Services) shared his views on the history of the Indian bond market and way forward. He quotes, “India’s bond market is much smaller than the US and China. For the market to grow, financial products must reach smaller towns and regions. Moreover, investor education is crucial for expanding bond market reach.”In another panel discussion, Rajkiran Rai G (MD, NaBFID) discussed on how bond markets can support India’s infrastructure financing needs, with a focus on scaling municipal bond issuances, creating sustainable funding channels for urban development, preparing urban local bodies for bond markets, and building wider participation in India’s fixed income market.The event concluded with a closing keynote by Ashish Kumar Chauhan (MD & CEO, NSE) focusing on the future of fixed income investing, capital market development, digital infrastructure, market access and the role of participation in deepening India’s bond market.Check out the https://ms.cnbctv18.com/india-fixed-income-summit/ for more insightful conversations, expert perspectives, and actionable insights that decode the rapidly transforming fixed income landscape in India.The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Loopring Shuts Down Decentralised Exchange, Halts Trading Operations
description: Loopring will distribute final user balances after ending DEX operations
content: Loopring has shut down its decentralised exchange and automated market maker after citing weak adoption, limited business development, and increasing competition from newer zkEVM solutions. The Ethereum scaling pioneer, which launched one of the first zk-rollup implementations, said it will distribute users’ remaining balances to their Ethereum addresses. The closure adds to a growing list of crypto projects winding down operations in 2026 amid changing market conditions.

title: Want more innovation? Stronger trade secret laws help
description: The conversation around intellectual property (IP) is typically dominated by patents and copyright, but there is one often overlooked pillar: trade secrets.Trade secrets represent over US$5 trillion in value and roughly two-thirds of all intangible assets in the United States alone. They are also, by definition, invisible. A study Colleen Cunningham and I recently published in Management Science provides rare, granular evidence on how firms manage confidential information in high-stakes environments.Our findings suggest that stronger trade secret protection can induce firms to innovate even when no registered IP, like patents, is claimed. What’s more, it can spur firms to share innovations with other companies.A quasi-natural experiment in the oil patchManagers face a persistent dilemma that can be called a “knowledge leakage trap”: The more valuable an innovation is, the riskier it may be to use, because deploying a new process or recipe requires sharing it with employees, contractors or partners who may eventually take that knowledge to a competitor. One CEO we interviewed put it bluntly: “Our trade secrets are constantly getting passed around.”This fear results in firms holding back their most advanced inventions. The problem is particularly acute in sectors where employee mobility and uneven bargaining power between partners are common. Besides oil and gas, such sectors include chemical, cosmetic and food products, semiconductors, and advanced manufacturing.Our study looks at over 47,000 hydraulic fracturing wells in the US between 2014 and 2018. In certain US states, stringent regulations require firms to disclose the chemical ingredients in their fracturing fluids unless those ingredients are substantiated trade secrets. This creates rare documentation of actual trade secret use at the level of individual wells.The Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA) served as the external shock to trade secret protection for our study. This was the first US federal legislation to create a unified jurisdiction for trade secret cases in the country. Legal experts at the time described it as the most significant expansion of federal intellectual property law in at least 30 years. By comparing how firms in states with weaker pre-existing trade secret protections responded to the DTSA against those in states where protection was already stronger, we could isolate the effect of strong protection.We found that in states where the legal change represented the biggest shift – Arkansas, Louisiana and Pennsylvania – the proportion of wells using at least one trade secret ingredient rose by 22 percentage points after the DTSA came into force. The share of ingredients kept secret per well increased by 3.7 percentage points (from a baseline of 2.2%). Not only did firms introduce new secret ingredients at higher rates, but they also didn’t use fewer disclosed novel ingredients or reduce their patenting activity. Wells with trade secret ingredients produced, on average, 26% more oil and 6% more gas than those without.Perhaps the most counterintuitive finding was that firms ostensibly shared more secret information with other firms after the law changed, as measured by sourcing trade secrets from third parties. In other words, firms trusted that the legal environment would help protect them from leaks. The conventional assumption is that stronger trade secret protection means more hoarding. Our data suggest the opposite may be true.What this means for executivesSecrecy tends to work best where reverse engineering is difficult, when the pace of technological change is fast, and where patenting is not the automatic default. Chemical formulations, algorithms, manufacturing processes and production techniques all fit that profile.Also Read: How text and data drive the future of copyright in AIOur research also suggests that secrecy and patenting aren’t mutually exclusive. Increased trade secret use among the firms in our sample did not come at the cost of disclosed innovation. Instead, firms used more secrets and introduced more novel disclosed ingredients, and didn’t patent less.Treating secrecy as an afterthought to a patents-first strategy means leaving significant value unprotected and, in many cases, unused. In fact, rather than viewing trade secrets as a passive alternative to patents, executives can use them as a catalyst for experimentation and competitive advantage.Our study suggests several ways to do so:Leverage trade secrecy in high-leakage environments: Stronger legal frameworks like the DTSA allow firms to increase trade secret use, particularly in regions where non-compete enforcement is low or competition is stiff. The European Union’s Trade Secrets Directive serves a similar purpose.Prioritise secrecy for "complex recipes": Inventions that involve a combination of ingredients or processes – which are harder to reverse engineer but valuable and hence tempting for a partner to misappropriate – benefit most from trade secret protection.Secrecy as a catalyst: Managers should exploit legal protections to move innovations out of the lab and into real-world applications where they can reap immediate ROI. Contrast this with patenting, where approval could take years. To a large extent, this is why firms in the business of fast-moving technologies such as software have relied heavily on secrecy and trade secret protection. An example is the recent pivot by Alphabet’s DeepMind to more secretive measures.Complement patenting with secrecy: Secrecy doesn't have to replace patenting or other types of disclosures. In fact, we found that firms often increased their use of both disclosed and secret novel inputs post-DTSA. This suggests that a robust secrecy strategy can enhance a firm’s broader innovation portfolio.A book chapter I recently co-wrote provides further detailed advice to managers on how to create and capture value from their business secrets.Secrecy and openness can complement each otherPrior research has suggested that stronger reliance on secrecy could dampen the flow of knowledge that feeds invention, leading to fewer patents filed and less disclosures for rivals to build on. Our findings suggest that the picture is more nuanced.What we observed was more experimentation, not less. Bolstered by stronger legal protection, firms deployed novel recipes, sourced more broadly, disclosed new inputs more, and didn’t retreat from patenting. Secrecy and openness, it turns out, are not as opposed as one might think.

title: The future is electric. But when that future is… that is difficult to predict
description: In this era of technological upheavals, the signals emanating from the global automotive industry could leave you confused. When it comes to choosing which to focus on—internal combustion, electric vehicles, hybrids or plug-in hybrids—it is pretty much different strokes for different folks. In the midst of this, Mercedes-Benz is keeping its mind, and options, wide open. Mathias Geisen, who joined the company’s global board of management in February 2025 and looks after sales and customer experience, explains to Forbes India why the German luxury vehicles giant is letting the customer choose. Excerpts from an interview edited for clarity and brevity:Q. As the global head of sales and customer experience, how do you see the electric segment of the luxury car market evolving in India?India is a very interesting market. If you look at the so-called top-end segment, we have expanded globally from 11 percent in 2019 to 15 percent last year. That is a pretty strong number and important for us because we want to keep those customers with our brand for long. In this top-end segment, we invest a lot to have new products, new craftsmanship options, to make sure those customers appreciate what we offer them.If you now look at India, that 15 percent is 25 percent—a much higher share of top-end sales. So, it is not just that we have been market leaders for the last 11 years in a row in India, it is also that in this top-end segment, we are very, very well-positioned.In the luxury segment, in general, worldwide, which is pretty comparable to India, on average roughly 10 percent is electric. This is heavily increasing because of the new products we have launched in Europe—they will come to other regions as well, but we first launched them in Europe. We are sitting at twice that number and going in the right direction.In India, it is interesting to see that electric is 20 percent in the top-end segment. So, in the top-end segment there is strong interest in battery electric vehicles.Q. Where do you see the new buyers coming from? Are your buyers getting younger? Is their thinking different?Talking about the customer… big difference. In India, the customers are much younger. The average customer in India is at the end of his 30s. Rest of the world, excluding China, it is around, plus or minus 50. The customers come from another generation, which has grown up on technology and is willing to invest in something substantial. Which is why we see this premiumisation. They want more equipment in the vehicle. They appreciate new technology and the craftsmanship in their cars. This is the sophistication we see specially in India. So, when we launch a new product for this market, we look specifically at the specifications for markets where customer demand is slightly different from the global requirement.Also Read: JLR bets on hybrids and US as it looks to get back on growth pathQ. What would be the average age in China of a Mercedes buyer?Also below 40. Globally, if you look at the automotive landscape, nobody knows for sure which way things are going. For instance, Ferrari launched the Luce.I am happy that two weeks earlier we launched our AMG GT electric and received a lot of feedback. It is really…. you got to drive it. Everyone who has been in this vehicle… with its performance… more than 1,100 horsepower (1,169). This is really amazing. [It’s a] very new segment, these high-end electric sports cars.Q. Another high-end player, Lamborghini, does not want to do electric. What was meant to be its first electric car will now come as a plug-in hybrid. You are also big on plug-in hybrids. How do you see the mix of powertrains evolving in India?I think coming up with a forecast when it comes to electric mobility is difficult. I was head of corporate strategy five years ago and I definitely overestimated the speed with which customers will be willing to switch to electric mobility. On the other hand, now that we have new products launched in Europe, it is incredible how the pickup rates are. More than 40 percent of the order intake in Europe now is fully electric. I’m not speaking of plug-in hybrids but fully electric. It shows for the right products, with great design and great technology and great everyday usability… now there is a strong pull in the market for those products.It is still too difficult to say how they will develop market to market. That is why we have this strategic flexibility in our powertrain offerings. There will be markets where combustion engines will play a role in the 2030s. So, we cannot stop offering something. We have to build what our customers want. That means we have high complexity now. We offer petrol engines, diesel engines, all electrified, we offer plug-in hybrids and battery electric vehicles as well. But we have to do that because market requirements are so, so different. Even if the markets are right next to each other. Norway, Sweden and Denmark, for example, are completely different, heavily affected by regulations as well.So, we make sure the customer can get the same iconic design of the car and can choose what powertrain they want. We also believe for India plug-in hybrids can be an interesting alternative, which helps especially in those markets where the infrastructure may not be where it could already be. That is why plug-in hybrids were very strong in Europe. But now, with the increase of infrastructure in Europe, and, in parallel, the new offerings, battery electric vehicles have started to outperform plug-in hybrids.We are not teachers, we don’t want to educate the customers. The customer has to decide what he wants to have. We will make sure we have attractive offerings for him. The electric Mercedes-AMG GT 4-door coupé was officially revealed in late May. Photo courtesy Mercedes-BenzQ. But your target of going all electric by 2039 remains in place.Absolutely. Ambition 2039 remains in place. We also believe the future is electric. But when that future is… that is difficult to predict.Also Read: Can Mahindra do an SUV in EVs?Q. Any great marketing success works because you tell a story. What is the story electric vehicles in your market segment have to tell?At the beginning of electrification, years back, the customers wanted to show to the outside world they drove something different, that they were very much into new technology. They were the early adopters. That is why our early electric cars looked different from the combustion engine cars.Drastically different. That was the right assumption in those days, but that has changed. Over the years the customer said, ‘You know what, we love the technology. It is quiet, there is immediate torque, it is attractive from total cost of ownership, etc. But why does it have to look different from your beautiful combustion engine vehicles?’This is why we are, step by step, closing the zipper because customer requirements have changed. The early adopters were more into expressing, ‘I want to have something different’. Now they have kind of balanced out and we are more in a phase where, pretty much globally, customers say, ‘Hey, just make the vehicles look great, but I will tell you what powertrain I want to have’.Q. You have followed a philosophy of value over volume for at least four years. How is that panning out when your primary competitor is going hard at volumes with an entry point lower than yours?We call it profitable growth. Of course, we want to grow, but in a profitable and sustainable way. Our customers—this is pretty much a global pattern—say they buy a Mercedes-Benz because it has reasonable residual value and that is important for them, that they buy something that is still worth something after a while.Our intention is not just to push volumes for the sake of having big numbers and market share. We want to do that with a balanced approach to profitably and sustainably grow the business.Q. When you say profitable growth, is the emphasis on growth or profits?(Laughs) Within the company, people have been asking that question for 30 years. I have been with the company since 1998. And since 1998 we have said we want to grow profitably. And every employee asks, ‘What do you want now? Do you want profit or do you want to grow?’We indeed want to do both, and that works. You can grow without doing everything just for the sake of growing. It also means you need strategic patience. I cannot come to India and say it is a sprint. It is a marathon. You got to get rolling, you got to continuously invest, you have ups and downs, but you will finally win the marathon if you do it with a long-term perspective. It will be wrong to just push out volumes to risk residual values, risk lease rates, just to say, ‘Hey, I sold some thousand units more than you’. You have to do that in a reasonable and balanced way. We have to create a pull in the market with the right products and I’d say we are on a good track with that.

title: Ray Dalio was a ‘below average’ student who got into investing by caddying for Wall Street traders: Now he hires talent who have experienced hardship
description: “What I find quite often is the case is that that student who did really, really well—and in remembering all the things that they’ve learned and so on—hadn’t gone through anything like that, [and] may not be the most inventive, may not be the most determined.”
content:
Ray Dalio was a self-professed “below average” student, bringing home grades of C-minus on a good day. However, the billionaire investor is now a household name, and says when he is looking to hire talent, he seeks out people who have had to overcome obstacles to get opportunities. In a conversation with Harvey Schwartz, CEO of the Carlyle Group, the Bridgewater Associates founder outlined how his early years were no indication of the success he later attained. High school was a struggle, he said, because he had a “lousy” memory, and classes involved teachers merely testing that memory.In addition, Dalio said, he simply wasn’t interested in much of the content, and instead kept himself busy by working odd jobs.



“I caddied,” Dalio said on the CNN podcast. “And when I would walk around the golf course caddying for people, the stock market was hot at the time. So I would talk about stocks with them.



“I took my caddy money, and I put it into the stock market. First stock I bought was the only company I ever heard of that was selling for less than $5 a share. And my logic was I could buy more shares, so if it went up, I’d make more money, which was dumb.”



That company (Dalio has previously said it was Northeast Airlines, which he first bought at age 12) soon went bankrupt. However, “some other company acquired it, it tripled in value, and I got hooked,” Dalio noted. Northeast Airlines, based in Boston, was purchased by Delta in 1972.



Dalio previously told the sports media outlet Golf that the club in question was the Links Golf Club on Long Island, which no longer exists, and that one of the men he caddied for was Donald Stott of Wagner, Stott & Co., a specialist Wall Street trading firm. Another was George Leib, who had served as the chairman of investment banking house, Blyth & Co.



Dalio’s hiring ethos



Dalio fared better in college (he attended C.W. Post College of Long Island University), saying he was more engaged because he could choose his own courses.



However, having experienced academia as a slog, Dalio now looks to hire people who have also experienced some setbacks in life.



He explained: “I get to hire the best of the best out of whatever schools I want, the best and the brightest. And what I find quite often is the case is that that student who did really, really well—and in remembering all the things that they’ve learned and so on—hadn’t gone through anything like that, [and] may not be the most inventive, may not be the most determined.”



Younger job seekers in the U.S. are facing a complex labor market at present: The St Louis Fed’s latest reading for May 2026 found unemployment for 16- to 24-year-olds was 9.4%, an improvement on mid-2025, but still elevated compared with previous years.



Experts have told Fortune that the job market is changing year to year for young people, and with the emergence of AI, so too have the skills required to land a job. 



But Dalio isn’t necessarily looking for a skill, but rather a quality. “There’s a lot of talent out there,” he said. “There’s a lot of people who are talented in the circumstances where there’s barriers, and they got past their barriers. That’s quite good.”
This story was originally featured on Fortune.com

title: NASA's X-59 "frankenjet" tests supersonic flight without the sonic boom
description: NASA’s quiet supersonic flight tests could eventually go on a national tour.
content:
More than two decades since the Concorde supersonic airliner last took to the skies, NASA has been flying an experimental aircraft designed to replace loud sonic booms with a quieter thump equivalent to a car door slamming shut 20 feet away. A successful NASA flight test program could influence the design of future supersonic airliners capable of flying overland routes without rattling buildings—and people’s nerves.
The Lockheed Martin X-59 Quesst—an acronym for Quiet SuperSonic Technology—first took flight late last year and recently began supersonic test flights. But unlike with many experimental “X-plane” aircraft that may never leave restricted airspace near Edwards Air Force Base in California, NASA plans to eventually take the X-59 on a tour around the United States so residents of various cities and towns can provide feedback on the quieter sonic “thumps” it produces.
“Usually an X plane is kind of bare-bones—‘cobble it together from a bunch of parts from other airplanes and just demonstrate one thing,’” said Jim “Clue” Less, a NASA test pilot and aerospace engineer, in an interview with Ars. “We need to demonstrate that one thing, but then we need a plane that's robust enough that we can fly it all over the place and gather that data.”Read full article

title: Thieves continue to target VW badges after a decade of problems
description:
Hundreds of badges have been stolen to date as thieves target valuable sensor packages

Ten years since the first reports emerged, grille badges that incorporate the radar unit used by their ADAS systems are still being stolen from some Volkswagen vehicles, with victims paying out hundreds of pounds in repairs.
In 2016, owners took to social media to report how thieves were prising the grille badges from the latest VW models such as the Mk7 Golf to gain access to the radar unit that controls features including the vehicle’s adaptive cruise control and autonomous emergency braking system.
More recently, in May 2024, The Guardian newspaper reported that the badges and radar units were still being stolen, with more than 50 residents in one London borough falling victim to the crime.
However, despite the fact that the radar unit cannot be reused without being electronically coded to the vehicle by VW before being accurately calibrated, the badges and the radar units they conceal continue to be taken two years later.
Among the latest victims is Claire Coleman. The grille badge on her VW Passat 2.0 TDI, registered in 2020, was stolen while the vehicle was parked on a street close to her home in Brixton, south London. “It was there one day and gone the next,” she said.
Neighbours quickly shared their stories of similar thefts, with one claiming his car had been targeted twice. Coleman reported the theft to the police.

Without the radar unit, her car’s adaptive cruise control, speed limiter and lane assist did not function. In addition, a warning light indicating a fault with the ACC and the error message ‘Front Assist not available’ was displayed, although these would not cause the vehicle to fail its MOT.
A VW dealer quoted almost £2000 to replace the badge and radar unit but told Coleman that, owing to a customer support package made available by Volkswagen in response to badge thefts, it might be possible to reduce the bill to around £400.
The precise value of the contribution is decided on a case-by-case basis and Coleman was eventually quoted £539.
A former VW technician told Autocar that in similar cases, the size of VW’s contribution may be linked to a car’s service history, with full main dealer history attracting greater support. Coleman’s car has a partial main dealer history.
“The cost is one thing but it’s the fact that VW has not publicised the fact that these radar units are single-use devices that cannot simply be fitted to another VW that frustrates me,” said Coleman. “If they did, it might put off the thieves.”
Coleman has since had her car repaired but, when not using it, Blu-Tacks a black plastic cover over the badge and parks as close to other cars as possible.
A spokesman for VW said that to date, only a few hundred badges had so far been stolen, with thefts concentrated mainly within limited areas of London.
He confirmed the company is offering financial assistance to customers through its dealer network and advised those affected to log the incident with the police and with their local Volkswagen retailer. Both these actions would, he said, assist with making further thefts less likely.
On the question of making the single-use nature of the radar unit more widely known and thereby perhaps discouraging further thefts, the spokesman said VW was reluctant to publicise the crime for fear of increasing it.

title: The goal now is to create a legacy: Neeraj Chopra
description: It’s not often that Neeraj Chopra leaves a competition without a medal. Before he placed eighth at the 2025 World Championships in Tokyo, where he was the defending champion, Chopra had finished in the top two at 26 consecutive international competitions—a streak that included two Olympic Games and two World Championships. He later revealed that he had carried a back injury in Tokyo, which forced him into a nearly nine-month layoff.When he returned to competition at the Doha Diamond League earlier this month, Chopra finished fourth, again falling short of the lofty standards he has set for himself. But the 28-year-old believes there’s a silver lining this time. In an interview with Forbes India from Switzerland, where he is currently training, he shares his path to recovery and why he is optimistic about the upcoming Commonwealth Games (CWG). Edited excerpts from the interview:Q. 85.69m, which you threw at the Doha Diamond League, isn’t your best. But given this was your return to competition, what does this performance mean to you?Initially, I wanted to wait a little for the comeback because I had resumed throwing javelins only in May. So the Doha competition came a bit too quickly for me. Why did I still participate in Doha? I had earlier thought that I would directly go to the CWG, that begins in July. But in the Indian Athletics Series that took place in Ludhiana in June, two javelin throwers—Rohit Yadav and Yashvir Singh—qualified for the Games by breaching the 82.61m mark. And then I also felt that I would like to go to the CWG only if I met the qualification mark. Because I haven’t played since the Tokyo World Championships last September, I myself don’t feel good about getting a direct entry without proving myself. And the Athletics Federation of India also said that I needed to play at least once. That’s why, just a week before Doha, I decided to compete.Q. What were your thoughts before returning to competition after such a long break?Before taking the decision to compete in Doha, I did a session once in training. It felt good and I thought I could do it. Yes, it was not my best throw at the competition, although my attempt was to come back very well. But, in sports, you can’t always have your way; sometimes you have to let time and situation dictate things. And considering those two factors, I thought the throw was quite good.I had thrown only for a month before Doha. In the first session after resuming, I threw with a lot of fear and could cover only about 60-63m. After that, in the next session, I increased it by 3-4m. By Doha, I quickly crossed 85m. I was quite happy to have come this far in a month. I now have about a month until the CWG, where I will train more and move forward.Q. Now that you've completed your first tournament, which areas do you feel you need to work on to get back to your peak form?First, I will have to throw more. Because my throwing sessions have reduced a lot. The more I throw, the more confidence I will get, the more I will be able to work on my mistakes.Rest, I have to work on my fitness to be able to train a little more. So, in the next two to three weeks, I will do a bit of intense training so that [my] body returns to the condition it was in a year or two ago, and I can gain speed and strength.I typically do two throwing sessions a week, but now I have to focus more on intensity and technique. For example, if I do 10-15 throws now, I will try and add 8-10 extra throws. In javelin, you can’t practise as many throws as in shot put, discus, etc. Here, aside from the throw, you have to run, you have to block (the sudden stop before the javelin is thrown). The block puts a lot of force on the body, so two sessions a week are enough.Q. One of the most challenging things for an athlete is staying away from the sport. You battled a series of injuries over nine months—shoulder, ankle, back and elbow. How did you keep yourself motivated?I am a javelin thrower and if I can’t throw a javelin at some time, it indeed is very difficult. Normally, we start throwing in January or February, but this time I only started in May. But one thing is that I have done it a few times. I’ve had injuries before, I’ve had surgeries, I have had to rehab [undergo rehabilitation] for a year, so I know that when you have an injury, it will keep you away from competitions. The other thing is that I wasn’t on bed rest. So if I am moving, if I am going to the gym, I see improvement every day and I realise something is happening. I have a daily target of improving from what I was yesterday. I want to gain more strength, so I always have a goal in mind.Q. Is there anything you learnt about your body over the past nine months that you didn’t realise before?When I was younger, I would recover faster, and I didn't take recovery so seriously. As I am growing older, I am starting to take recovery more seriously. I have been throwing for many years, I have been competing at the international level for a long time now. Which means I have been pushing my body for years. Now, I know when to stop pushing—it’s something I didn’t know earlier. That way, now I know more about my body. If I feel something is off, I stop, speak to my coach and physio, and work on it.Q. The CWG is just a month away. Olympic champion Arshad Nadeem will be there, as will World No. 2 Rumesh Pathirage and No. 3 Anderson Peters. How are you preparing for this field?The competition is going to be tough. There will be at least five or six throwers who have breached the 90m mark. As I said, I will focus on intense training, throws and technique for two to three weeks. I can't do anything new right now since I don’t have much time. But I will work on the areas where I can push myself further and the target is to take to the field 100 percent injury-free. When I get fit, I enjoy pushing myself and I get what I want.Q. Last year, you threw 90m for the first time. That must have been a big relief after being asked about that mark for years?It’s in our mind that 90m is a magical mark and that it’s a must in our sport. But if you ask for my opinion, there is a big difference between throwing 90m and winning the Olympics or World Championships. For me, competing with the world’s elite athletes, throwing your best under pressure, and winning top competitions is a bigger deal than just throwing 90m. A long throw and a medal are two different things.Q. So, for you, 90m is just a number…Yes. If I win a gold without 90m, that is a better result for me than throwing 90m only to end up with a bronze or fourth place. Distances don’t matter; medals do. You have to prove yourself on that day, so the important thing is to throw better than every other competitor, be it 90m or not.Q. Before injury took you off the field last year, you finished in the top two in 26 consecutive international competitions. How did you maintain such consistency?The main thing is to push your body and mind with training. When my throw lagged behind that of other athletes, I just wouldn’t accept that performance. My target was to always throw around my personal best. I have to admit that [maintaining] consistency over the years was tough, but if I was fit and doing my best, it could be done. Last year, when I had an injury during the Tokyo World Championships and I couldn’t perform well, I realised how difficult it was to maintain that consistency.Q. You’ve now returned to coaching with Jaiveer Choudhary, who was one of your earliest inspirations. How have the two of you chalked out the road ahead?We have known each other for a long time and share a certain level of comfort. Even when I was training with other coaches, I would still talk to him. Over the past few years, I have trained with international coaches like Uwe Hohn, Klaus Bartonietz and Jan Železný, some of whom are legends of the sport. Who could I even pick after them? So, I decided to work on my own ideas and since I share that comfort level with Jai Bhai, I started working with him. I’m happy and I feel we are moving in the right direction—as you saw, even though we had a really short prep [preparation] time for Doha, we did well.Q. You’ve been an Olympic champion, a World Champion, you’ve won the Diamond League… practically everything that a javelin thrower dreams of. What else are you hungry for?Now, my target is to improve my throws, to be consistent. I have achieved 90m, but now I should go even bigger. The hunger is to create a legacy. I have won these titles, abhi baar baar jeetna hai (now I have to win repeatedly). When we look at the likes of Usain Bolt and Michael Phelps, they have proved themselves every time they have competed. That is my target too.
